name: OC Abundance Project
tagline: Remove the barriers. Let people build families.
mission:
Society should make it radically easy for people who want children to have them. The barriers to family formation — unaffordable childcare, scarce family-sized housing, nonexistent parental leave, crumbling school infrastructure — are policy failures, not personal failures. Fix the policy, and people who want families can build them.

This is the same mission as the Austin Abundance Project, adapted to Orange County's specific context — where the barriers are even more acute:

• OC childcare costs average $1,404/month for daycare, $1,100-1,777/month for preschool. Waitlists are months long. Large areas are childcare deserts.

• First 5 Orange County is the major early childhood funder — $50M+ annual budget from Prop 10 tobacco tax revenue. This is real money that needs organized advocacy to ensure it's spent effectively on expanding capacity, not just maintaining existing programs.

• Irvine ranks #4 nationally for raising a family — but the costs are astronomical. Median home prices are $1.2M-$1.6M. A family-sized home (3BR+) is $1.5M+ in most OC cities. You can't have a second kid in a one-bedroom apartment at $2,800/month rent.

• The housing-family connection is even stronger here than in Austin. OC's median home price is 10-15x median household income. Family formation requires either generational wealth, dual high incomes, or leaving the county entirely.

• UCI Health is a major employer with 16,000+ employees. It and other OC healthcare/biotech employers (Edwards Lifesciences, Allergan, Broadcom, Western Digital, Blizzard) are the equivalent of Austin's tech sector — persuadable on family benefits through competitive talent pressure.

We treat childcare, parental leave, family-sized housing, and pediatric healthcare as INFRASTRUCTURE problems with engineering solutions, not culture war issues. This is emphatically not about traditional family structures exclusively — single parents, same-sex couples, non-traditional families all face the same infrastructure failures.
theory_of_change: Family formation barriers in Orange County are concentrated in a few specific, addressable policy domains: childcare facility access and cost, employer benefits, and housing supply/affordability. First 5 OC's $50M+ annual budget creates an immediate accountability opportunity — organized advocates can influence how that money is spent. Employer transparency campaigns work because OC's healthcare and tech labor markets are competitive — companies respond to talent retention pressure. And the housing connection means we amplify the YIMBY movement's work while adding a constituency — parents and prospective parents — that politicians find very sympathetic. The California state framework (paid family leave, childcare licensing, housing mandates) provides stronger baseline protections than Texas, but local implementation gaps remain enormous.

political_positioning.description: Center-left economically — we believe in public investment in childcare and family infrastructure. Libertarian-leaning socially — we do not moralize about family structure and support all family types. Explicitly not aligned with religious-right pro-natalism. Explicitly not aligned with anti-tech or anti-growth perspectives. Broad coalition: healthcare workers, tech workers, working parents, childcare providers, urbanists, and employers. In OC's purple political environment, the pro-family framing bridges partisan divides more effectively than many other policy frames.

key_policy_positions: Reform childcare facility zoning to expand home-based and small-center childcare in residential areas across OC cities
Monitor and advocate for effective deployment of First 5 Orange County's $50M+ annual budget on capacity expansion
Advocate for employer childcare benefits and paid parental leave at OC's major employers (UCI Health, Edwards Lifesciences, Broadcom, Western Digital, Blizzard)
Push for family-sized unit requirements or incentives (2BR+) in new housing developments, coordinating with OC Housing Now
Research and publish employer transparency data on parental leave and family benefits for OC's top employers
Support co-housing and innovative family housing models appropriate to OC's high-cost market
Advocate for urban school quality in Orange Unified and Irvine Unified to prevent family suburban flight
Connect family-sized housing advocacy to broader RHNA compliance and housing supply campaigns
Track and publicize California's paid family leave utilization rates in OC to identify access gaps
Partner with UCI Health and other major employers to establish on-site or subsidized childcare

active_campaigns.title: OC Childcare Desert Mapping & Advocacy
active_campaigns.summary:
Use California Community Care Licensing data + Census ACS data (children under 5) + First 5 OC investment data to map childcare deserts across Orange County. Identify where licensed childcare capacity is insufficient relative to the population of young children. Track how First 5 OC's $50M+ annual budget is being deployed — is it expanding capacity in deserts or maintaining existing programs in already-served areas? Advocate for childcare facility zoning reform in cities with the worst gaps.

OC has unique dynamics: high demand (affluent families competing for spots), high costs ($1,404/month average for daycare), and significant geographic variation (coastal cities vs. inland cities, newer developments vs. older neighborhoods). The desert map makes this visible and actionable.

⚡ MAY 17 UPDATE: ⚡ VOTER REGISTRATION DEADLINE PASSED (May 18). The June 2 primary shapes who controls childcare policy at county and state levels. The next governor controls California's childcare mandates. The Board of Supervisors controls the county's $10.8B budget including childcare funding. Builder's Remedy applications in OC (696 units in Orange alone) will create new residential density that needs childcare infrastructure — the desert mapping should incorporate planned RHNA development sites to anticipate future demand.

⚡ MAY 21 UPDATE: 10 DAYS to June 2 primary. PIT COUNT RESULTS (May 19): OC homelessness down 13.7% to 6,321 — 882 seniors and 245 youth counted. Family instability is a pathway into homelessness — childcare and family infrastructure prevent crises upstream. The OC Streetcar (95% complete, August 1 launch target) will reshape transit access in Santa Ana/Garden Grove — childcare desert mapping should incorporate the 10 streetcar station areas as priority zones for new capacity. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH fined $160K for housing noncompliance, escalating to $50K/month — the enforcement framework enabling new family housing depends on the governor's race.

⚡ MAY 24 UPDATE: 9 DAYS to June 2 primary. The Board of Supervisors race and governor's race determine who controls childcare policy at county and state levels. FY2026-27 Recommended Budget released May 20 for BOS review — childcare allocations are part of the $10.8B annual budget. Huntington Beach compliance deadline May 28 (4 DAYS) — the housing enforcement framework enables new family housing.

⚡ MAY 25 UPDATE: 8 DAYS to June 2 primary. ⚡ The 166-unit affordable senior housing community 'The Orion' opened in the City of Orange — a $63.6M public-private partnership including First 5 OC-adjacent funding streams. While senior-focused, this model demonstrates the public-private partnership approach needed for family childcare infrastructure. Childcare costs in Orange County average $819/month for full-time care (daycare) but up to $1,385/month in the City of Orange — consuming 15% of a married couple's median income and 47% for a single parent. The HHS affordability standard is 7%. Huntington Beach compliance deadline May 28 (3 DAYS) — new housing enabled by enforcement needs childcare infrastructure.

⚡ MAY 26 UPDATE: 7 DAYS to June 2 primary. Huntington Beach compliance deadline May 28 (2 DAYS) — new housing enabled by enforcement needs childcare infrastructure. The Board of Supervisors race and governor's race determine who controls childcare policy at county and state levels. Memorial Day — campaigns at lower intensity but mail ballots continue arriving.

**May 27 update:** 6 DAYS to June 2 primary. ⚡ OC PIT COUNT: Homelessness down 13.7% to 6,321 — 882 seniors and 245 youth counted. Family instability remains a pathway into homelessness; childcare and family infrastructure prevent crises upstream. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH: Compliance deadline TOMORROW (May 28) — $50K/month fines start June. New housing enabled by enforcement needs childcare infrastructure planning. ⚡ County budget pressures from Airport Fire payouts, sexual harassment settlements, and federal funding cuts threaten family-serving programs in the $10.8B annual budget. The Board of Supervisors and governor's races determine who controls these allocations.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: HUNTINGTON BEACH COMPLIANCE DEADLINE IS TODAY — new housing enabled by enforcement needs childcare infrastructure alongside it. 5 DAYS until June 2 primary. OC PIT COUNT confirmed: 6,321 total (down 13.7%), first time more sheltered (3,256) than unsheltered (3,065) — but 882 seniors and 245 youth remain among the unhoused, highlighting family stability needs. County budget pressures continue from Airport Fire payouts and federal funding cuts.

**May 29 update:** ⚡ GARDEN GROVE CHEMICAL CRISIS — FAMILY IMPACT: GKN Aerospace's tank failure forced evacuation of 50,000 residents in Garden Grove and Stanton (May 21-26), including thousands of families with young children. Garden Grove Unified School District closed multiple campuses. Governor declared state of emergency. This is exactly the kind of event that exposes childcare infrastructure fragility — displaced families had nowhere to bring children while evacuated. The crisis highlights the need for emergency childcare planning alongside the desert mapping project. ⚡ 4 DAYS to June 2 primary. The Board of Supervisors race and governor's race determine who controls childcare policy. PPIC poll: Becerra 23%, Hilton 20% — the next governor controls California's childcare mandates and funding. ⚡ BOS ratified federal emergency declaration for the chemical crisis — county-level emergency response and recovery will be a major item for the next board.

**May 30 update:** ⚡ 3 DAYS to June 2 primary. Final weekend — in-person early voting opens at some locations. NEW UC BERKELEY IGS POLL (May 19-24): Becerra 25%, Hilton 21%, Steyer 19%. The race for second is tightening — a two-Democrat top-two (Becerra + Steyer) is now plausible, which would be favorable for childcare and family policy. ⚡ Garden Grove crisis aftermath: Governor Newsom initiated safety record review of GKN and similar chemical plants. The displaced-family impact highlights the need for emergency childcare planning — childcare desert mapping should now incorporate emergency preparedness zones (near industrial facilities) as a vulnerability layer. County budget pressures from crisis response costs threaten family-serving programs in the $10.8B annual budget.

⚡ MAY 31 UPDATE: 2 DAYS to June 2 primary. ALL VOTE CENTERS OPEN TODAY (10am-6pm). ⚡ FINAL EMERSON POLL (May 27-28): Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21% — Steyer has passed Hilton, making a two-Democrat top-two the most likely outcome. Both Becerra and Steyer support strong childcare and family policy investments. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH COMPLIANCE CONFIRMED per HCD — the housing plan was approved, ending 4.5+ years of noncompliance. New housing enabled by enforcement now needs childcare infrastructure planning alongside it. The 413 new affordable homes represent potential childcare demand — the desert mapping should track HB developments as a priority zone. ⚡ Garden Grove crisis aftermath: emergency childcare planning remains a gap. Thousands of families with young children were displaced during the May 21-26 evacuation. The crisis highlights the need for emergency childcare provisions in county emergency management plans.

**June 1 update:** CA PRIMARY TOMORROW. Garden Grove crisis displaced 50,000 — DA expanding criminal probe (drones, whistleblower tip line, record preservation). Multiple class-action lawsuits. Crisis exposed how childcare infrastructure collapses when communities disrupted. PIT Count down 13.7% (6,321). $20.9M NOFA + $35.1M HHAP = largest OC funding injection.

**June 3 update:** ⚡ PRIMARY RESULTS (June 2): D4 — Shaw (R) vs. Traut (D) advance to November (68-vote margin). D5 — Foley (D) leads Dixon (R). Governor — Hilton (R) 27% / Becerra (D) 26%. The FY2026-27 Recommended Budget ($10.5B) is under BOS review. Garden Grove chemical crisis cleanup continues under CUPA. OC Streetcar (92-95% complete) on track for August 2026 launch. Homelessness PIT count down 13.7% to 6,321 (first time more sheltered than unsheltered). The new D4 supervisor will inherit budget pressures from Airport Fire payouts, sexual harassment settlements, federal funding cuts, and Garden Grove crisis response costs.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: You can't fix what you can't see. A childcare desert map makes geographic disparities in childcare access visceral and concrete — every parent can see whether their neighborhood is served. First 5 OC funding accountability follows naturally: are they investing where the need is greatest? The map becomes the advocacy tool that enables targeted policy asks at specific city councils and the Board of Supervisors.
next_actions: Compile California Community Care Licensing provider database for Orange County
Pull Census ACS data on children under 5 by census tract for OC
Obtain First 5 OC investment data by program and geography
Overlay data to identify desert areas — census tracts with high child populations and low licensed capacity
Research childcare zoning regulations in key OC cities (Orange, Irvine, Santa Ana, Anaheim)
Draft desert map visualization for public distribution
Present findings to First 5 OC Commission as public comment

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of OC Childcare Desert Mapping & Advocacy often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If OC Childcare Desert Mapping & Advocacy is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move OC Childcare Desert Mapping & Advocacy forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: OC Family-Friendly Employer Index
active_campaigns.summary:
Research and publish a scorecard of Orange County's top 20 employers on family-friendliness: weeks of paid parental leave (birth parent and non-birth parent separately), childcare subsidy or on-site care, flexible work policies, fertility/adoption benefits, and return-to-work support. Target employers include UCI Health, Edwards Lifesciences, Broadcom, Western Digital, Blizzard Entertainment, Pacific Life, Hyundai NA, and others.

The competitive pressure theory works: when employers see their peers offering better family benefits, they improve to attract talent. UCI Health is uniquely positioned — as a university hospital system, it has both the resources and the mission alignment to lead on family benefits. If UCI Health leads, private employers follow.

**June 1 update:** CA PRIMARY TOMORROW. GKN Aerospace crisis raises employer accountability questions — safety failures exposed 50,000 residents. Employer disaster preparedness should be part of any responsible employer index.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Competitive pressure in tight labor markets drives employer behavior. A public ranking creates reputational stakes — no major employer wants to be last on the list. In OC's healthcare and tech sectors, where talent competition is intense, family benefits are a genuine competitive differentiator. Publishing the index before November 2026 elections also creates political context — candidates can be asked whether they support employer transparency requirements.
next_actions: Identify OC's top 20 employers by employee count and economic influence
Design scorecard methodology — categories, weighting, data collection approach
Send survey and public records requests to all target employers
Research publicly available benefits data (job postings, Glassdoor, benefits websites)
Draft scorecard format and ranking methodology
Identify media partners for index publication (Voice of OC, OC Register)
Time publication for Q3 2026 (before November elections)

active_campaigns.title: Family-Sized Housing Campaign
active_campaigns.summary:
Partner with OC Housing Now to ensure new housing developments include family-appropriate units — 2BR+ at minimum, ideally 3BR options. In OC's $1.2M-$1.6M median home price market, family-sized housing is the scarcest and most expensive segment. New apartments trend toward studios and 1BRs because they're cheaper to build and yield higher per-square-foot rents. This squeezes families out.

The campaign connects the housing supply fight to family formation: every development approved without family-sized units is a development that excludes families. Density bonuses for projects with 2BR+ unit minimums, reduced parking for family units near transit, and co-housing support are specific policy tools.

**June 1 update:** CA PRIMARY TOMORROW — Governor race affects housing enforcement. OC: 25,000 units built 2021-2024, only 17% affordable. Median home: $1,467,500. Saddleback Meadows (181 homes) approved unanimously. Oak Creek: 7-0 for zoning amendment + 3,100 homes. HB achieved compliance. D4/D5 races TOMORROW determine whether progressive housing majority holds.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: The housing supply fight and the family formation fight are the same fight. Making this connection explicit strengthens both movements. Developers include family-sized units when there are policy incentives (density bonuses) or requirements (unit mix mandates). Pro-family framing broadens the YIMBY coalition to include parents and prospective parents who might not identify as urbanists but who desperately need affordable family-sized housing.
next_actions: Analyze unit size distribution in recently approved OC developments
Research density bonus models that incentivize 2BR+ units in other California cities
Draft family housing incentive proposal for City of Orange and City of Irvine
Coordinate with OC Housing Now on joint advocacy for family-sized units in Builder's Remedy projects
Connect with Irvine Community Land Trust on family-oriented affordable housing models
Prepare public comment template connecting housing supply to family formation for city council hearings

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Family-Sized Housing Campaign often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

aligned_organizations.name: Austin Abundance Project
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-location ally. Same mission, different context. Austin fights for policy adoption; OC benefits from California's stronger state framework but faces higher costs. Share research methodology, employer index design, and childcare desert mapping approach.

aligned_organizations.name: OC Housing Now
aligned_organizations.relationship: Primary cross-movement ally. Every housing unit built is a family formation enabler. Our family-sized housing campaign directly connects to their supply advocacy. Joint advocacy amplifies both.

aligned_organizations.name: People for Housing OC
aligned_organizations.relationship: YIMBY ally — their housing advocacy supports family-sized housing goals. Coordinate on Builder's Remedy projects that include family units.

aligned_organizations.name: First 5 Orange County
aligned_organizations.relationship: Accountability target and potential partner. Their $50M+ annual budget is the largest childcare funding source in OC. We track and advocate for effective deployment.

aligned_organizations.name: UCI Health
aligned_organizations.relationship: Employer engagement target — 16,000+ employees. As a university hospital system, they have both the resources and mission alignment to lead on family benefits. Potential partner for on-site childcare and parental leave leadership.

aligned_organizations.name: OC Labor Federation
aligned_organizations.relationship: Labor ally — 200,000+ members across 90+ unions. Parental leave and childcare access are core labor issues. Their political influence amplifies our advocacy.

aligned_organizations.name: OC Purple Accountability
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Their scorecards help identify which elected officials support family infrastructure investment. Our research feeds their evaluation criteria.

engagement_pipeline.awareness: Social media, parenting groups, UCI community forums, employer network events. Publish childcare desert map and employer index for organic distribution. The family framing resonates across partisan lines in purple OC.
engagement_pipeline.interest: Check if your neighborhood is a childcare desert. See how your employer compares on family benefits. Read our research on OC's family formation barriers. Attend a First 5 OC Commission meeting.
engagement_pipeline.action: Submit public comment on childcare zoning at your city council. Share the employer index with your HR department. Attend a Board of Supervisors meeting when First 5 OC funding is on the agenda. Testify for family-sized housing at planning hearings.
engagement_pipeline.leadership: Join the research team. Lead childcare desert data analysis. Coordinate employer outreach for the index. Represent us at city council and Board of Supervisors meetings. Connect with Austin Abundance Project for cross-location strategy.

immediate.action: Complete CA Community Care Licensing database compilation for Orange County — childcare provider locations, capacity, license types
Send employer survey to OC's top 20 employers for family-friendly index
Follow up on Kennedy Commission 2026 Affordable Housing Awards (May 14 — PAST) — connect with family-serving organizations and track family-sized housing projects discussed

Voter registration deadline PASSED (May 18). Shift to GOTV — encourage early ballot return for June 2 primary (13 DAYS). Board of Supervisors majority directly affects childcare and family policy funding

medium_term.action: Complete childcare desert map v1 for Orange County
Present childcare desert findings to First 5 OC Commission
Draft family housing incentive proposal with OC Housing Now — include RHNA compliance data showing family-sized unit shortfalls
Track $20.9M OCHCD Supportive Housing NOFA awards — evaluate which projects include family-serving components

strategic.action: Publish OC Family-Friendly Employer Index v1
strategic.notes: Timed to create political context for November 2026 races. Candidates can be asked about employer transparency and childcare policy.

strategic.action: Advocate for childcare zoning reform at City of Orange and City of Irvine
strategic.notes: Use desert map data to make the case for zoning reform. Coordinate with OC Housing Now on joint advocacy.

strategic.action: Establish UCI Health as a family benefits leader — on-site childcare pilot
strategic.notes: UCI Health has the scale, mission, and resources to lead. If they establish on-site childcare, it creates competitive pressure on other major OC employers.

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Families may agree with pro-abundance goals but still fear that local plans overpromise: more housing can arrive before schools, parks, or childcare, and employer-friendly language may not translate into better wages or flexibility for workers.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Abundance should mean complete neighborhoods with housing, childcare, and family services together.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Family stability improves when childcare access and housing options are co-located near daily destinations.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We are not asking neighborhoods to carry growth alone—we are asking cities to plan complete, family-ready communities."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Family-friendly practices are talent strategy, not charity.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Retention and productivity improve when workers have predictable schedules, leave policies, and manageable commutes.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "A region that works for families is a region that keeps skilled workers."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Practical incremental wins beat ideological branding.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Pilot programs, scorecards, and phased zoning updates let communities evaluate outcomes before scaling.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "Let us start with measurable pilots and expand what demonstrably helps families."

common_ground: Most residents want Orange County to remain family-viable.
Policy should make everyday life less expensive and less stressful.
Public and private sectors both have roles in family stability.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Avoid framing abundance as anti-suburb or anti-homeownership.
Avoid implying employers alone can solve regional affordability.
Avoid one-size-fits-all claims across diverse OC cities.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: How quickly childcare deserts can close.
Market response to new family-sized zoning incentives.
Employer adoption rates for voluntary best-practice standards.
honest_limits: We cannot mandate private employer policy changes on our own.
We cannot fund large childcare expansions without partners.
We cannot guarantee short-term affordability gains in every city simultaneously.
